slides.title: Cleanup That Never Gets Skipped
slides.body: A **context manager** guarantees that cleanup code runs when you leave a block — even if an error crashes the party. Think of returning a library book: you *always* have to return it, no matter what happened while you were reading.
slides.tip: The `with` statement is Python's built-in way to use context managers.

slides.title: The Risky Way to Open Files
slides.body: Without a context manager, a crash between `open()` and `close()` leaves the file **permanently open** — wasting resources or corrupting data.
slides.code:
f = open("data.txt", "w")
f.write("Hello!")
# crash here → f.close() never runs!
f.close()
slides.tip: Unclosed files, sockets, and locks are common real-world bugs.

slides.title: `with` Makes It Bulletproof
slides.body: Wrap any resource in a `with` block and Python **automatically** calls cleanup when the block exits — crash or no crash.
slides.code:
with open("data.txt", "w") as f:
    f.write("Hello!")
# file is closed here — always
slides.tip: No `try/finally` needed — `with` handles it for you.

slides.title: Build Your Own: `__enter__` & `__exit__`
slides.body: Any class with `__enter__` (setup) and `__exit__` (teardown) can be a context manager. Here's a reusable **Timer**:
slides.tip: `__exit__` receives exception info — return `True` to suppress, `False` to let it propagate.

slides.title: Shortcut: `@contextmanager`
slides.body: No class needed! Use `@contextmanager` from `contextlib` — write a generator, put `yield` where the `with` block runs, and wrap it in `try/finally` for safe teardown.
slides.tip: Always use `try/finally` around `yield` — skipping it means errors can bypass your cleanup.

slides.title: You've Got Context Managers!
slides.body: You now know how `with` guarantees cleanup, how `__enter__`/`__exit__` power it under the hood, and how `@contextmanager` lets you write one in just a few lines. Files, locks, DB connections — **context managers keep them all tidy**.
slides.tip: Spot a `try/finally` for cleanup? It's probably a great candidate to become a context manager.
